fact: August 15 is exactly 227 days into the year, which means there are only 138 days left until New Year's Eve!
In the Northern Hemisphere, this is peak cricket season - male crickets are chirping their loudest to attract mates, and you can actually tell the temperature by counting their chirps!
The sun rises around 6:15 AM and sets around 7:45 PM in many northern cities, giving you over 13 hours of daylight to play outside.
August 15 falls right in the middle of Leo season, when the constellation Leo the Lion is most visible in the night sky.
In Australia and other Southern Hemisphere countries, August 15 is smack in the middle of winter, with temperatures that can drop below freezing!
This date is exactly 2 months before the autumn equinox in the north, when day and night become perfectly equal.
Monarch butterflies are typically in the middle of their epic migration south around this time in North America, traveling up to 100 miles per day!
In many places, August 15 brings the hottest nights of the year - the ground has been soaking up sun all summer and is now radiating heat back into the air.
Perseid meteor showers usually peak right around this date, meaning you might see up to 60 shooting stars per hour if you look up at the right time!
Corn plants in North America are typically at their absolute tallest around August 15 - some stalks can grow over 10 feet high, towering above most adults!
The moon phase on August 15 changes every year, but when it's a full moon on this date, it often appears extra orange and huge because of all the summer humidity in the air.
In northern forests, this is when squirrels go absolutely nuts collecting acorns and nuts for winter - they can bury up to 10,000 nuts in a single season!
